FY27 Strategic Architecture · 5 Pillars

Compounding Growth
🏗️
P1 · Distribution-Led Products
Build products for distribution we already own. Shift from push-driven acquisition to pull-driven embedded flows. CAC must decline structurally.
🔁
P2 · Repeat-First Engine
Growth from deepening relationships, not reacquiring. Known users get faster decisions, better pricing, and lower-friction journeys. Repeat is the business model.
⚖️
P3 · Risk-Adjusted Compounding
Move from absolute ROA to risk-adjusted ROA. Cohort-level decisioning, explicit caps on volatile segments. Predictability governs scale decisions.
🏦
P4 · Capital & Co-Lending Flywheel
Synthetic leverage from ₹1,500 Cr to ₹4,000 Cr+ at current equity. Own book proves performance; co-lending scales it.
🤖
P5 · AI-First Platform
Decision systems scale faster than teams. Majority of lending decisions automated. Marginal cost per ₹ lent declines at scale.
📋
AOP Coverage
Vision & Themes
100%
C2C + DCF
40%
Other BUs
5%

1️⃣ Why Lending?
2️⃣ Our Moats
3️⃣ Risk & Reward

5️⃣ Liquidity & Capital

Why Are We in the Lending Business?

Lending is the financial layer of the auto ecosystem — not an adjacent business
Core Thesis
In used-car finance, advantage comes from ecosystem control — not interest rate arbitrage.
CARS24 uniquely controls the asset, transaction moment, dealer network, and resale marketplace — creating structural lending leverage. If we don't lend, we leak margin at the highest-value node of our own ecosystem.

Four Structural Advantages — Why We Must Own This Layer

🚗
Asset Control
We own the car
Know the asset better than any lender — valuation, condition, history, blacklist
⚡
Transaction Moment
We own the intent
70% of buyers start their financing journey before finalizing the car — we catch them first
🤝
Dealer Network
1,500+ dealers
Integrated purchase, working capital, and consumer financing loop — 40%+ market share
🔄
Resale Marketplace
We own liquidation
Recovery at repossession 75% vs market 60-65% — structural NPA protection

Ecosystem Lending — Three Product Archetypes

All products are ecosystem-advantaged, though not all are distribution-captive
Product
Ecosystem Moat
Captivity Type
🛒
Retail (C2C Sale-Purchase)
Buyer and borrower journeys fully integrated within our transaction flow. 60% get pre-approved before test drive.
Transaction moment + pre-approval discovery + 90% share of wallet
Fully Captive
💳
DCF (Dealer Consumer Financing)
Dealers from our ecosystem — we know them and the cars. 40%+ market share despite open competition. 1,500 of 8,000+ active dealers.
Dealer liquidity + inventory + consumer financing loop = integrated flywheel
Ecosystem Captive
🔄
Refinance (LAC)
Asset valuation and liquidation confidence tied to our ecosystem. Superior repossession recovery vs all peers.
Asset intelligence + liquidation marketplace + proprietary valuation
Distribution + Intelligence Captive
Bottom Line
While not all products are distribution-captive, all are ecosystem-advantaged. We have structural leverage at every product level that no standalone lender can replicate.

Our Three Moats

Structural advantages that compound over time
📡
Moat 1 — Distribution Control
We own the customer at the highest-intent moment
C2C Retail Financing
🎯
Pre-intent capture
70% of buyers start their financing journey before finalizing the car — we are already in the funnel
⚡
Pre-approval discovery
60% of customers get pre-approved offers before their test drive — top-of-funnel catch
💰
90% share of wallet
Only 10% of customers take outside financing (used car / personal / gold loan)
Dealer Consumer Financing
🤝
Ecosystem dealers
1,500 of 8,000+ active dealers onboarded — we know them and their cars. Penetration growing.
🔄
Integrated flywheel
Purchase + working capital financing + consumer financing loop — 40%+ market share despite open competition
🧠
Moat 2 — Asset Intelligence
We know the car better than any lender in the market
⚡
Faster Underwriting
Instant online car valuation vs 1-day physical inspection required by all other lenders. Critical advantage in dealer ecosystem.
🎯
Precise Valuations
Accurate LTVs based on real asset data minimize losses at repossession. Market LTV 80% vs ours at 90% — higher confidence underwriting.
🚫
Fraud Prevention
Ecosystem data on all cars across 11 dimensions — service history, blacklisting, accidental records. 10% cars eliminated basis past data discrepancies.
Collection & Liquidation Moat — Peer Comparison
Lender LTV Offered Recovery at Repossession Loss at Default
CFSPL (Cars24) 🏠90%75%25%
Bajaj Finance75%65%35%
Piramal Finance80%60%40%
IDFC80%60%40%
Market Average80%60–65%40%
🏛️
Moat 3 — Two-Tiered Allocation Structure
Monetize distribution without taking full risk exposure — similar to CarMax's model
Given our control over distribution, we are able to segregate customer profiles based on risk and underwrite cohorts where we have comfort. We have built an ecosystem of 10+ lending partnerships (AU Bank, Kogta, Piramal Finance, Equitas, etc.) to leverage underwriting on a no-risk distribution model.
T1
Tier 1 — Super-prime & Prime
Underwritten in own books. Best risk cohorts with clean bureau, stable income, and high asset quality.
Full margin capture · Own NIM · High confidence
T2
Tier 2 — Sub-prime
Underwritten on partner books. Distribution fee model — we originate, partners underwrite and hold credit risk.
Fee income · Zero credit risk · 10+ partners
Lending Partnership Ecosystem
AU Bank Kogta Piramal Finance Equitas + 6 more partners

Risk & Reward Framework

How ecosystem moats translate into superior ROA
Transaction-Captive Business (C2C / Retail)
NIM advantageHigh
CoA (Cost of Acquisition)Near Zero
Asset quality riskLower
Share of wallet90%
Recovery at repossession75–78%
Ecosystem-Captive Business (DCF / Dealer)
Speed advantageInstant valuation
Market share40%+ active dealers
Asset knowledge11 data dimensions
Fraud prevention10% cars eliminated
LTV confidence90% vs 80% market

Why Our LTVs Can Be Higher — The Liquidation Advantage

90%
Our LTV
vs 80% market average. Higher LTV = more customers eligible, better conversion.
75–78%
POS Recovery at Liquidation
vs 60–65% peers. Our resale marketplace gives structural recovery advantage.
22–25%
Write-off Recovery (Lifetime)
vs 10–15% industry standard. Loss on Sale recovery program on write-off pool.
The Compounding Effect
Asset Intelligence
→ Better LTVs
Higher LTVs
→ More approvals
Resale Marketplace
→ Higher recovery
Higher Recovery
→ Lower loss = better ROA

Industry Firsts in Used Car Lending

Proprietary collection infrastructure that no competitor has replicated
📡
GPS on Every Car Disbursed
Industry first — GPS installed on every vehicle at disbursal. Significantly helps repossession and skip tracing. No other lender in used car finance does this at scale.
🚫
Blacklisting & Whitelisting
Proprietary asset registry. Cars with risk history are blacklisted across the ecosystem — prevents serial fraud, double-dipping, and bad asset recirculation.
🔄
Liquidation Moat
~75–78% POS recovery at liquidation with higher LTVs (~100%). 10–15% additional recovery from write-off pool post repossession (Loss on Sale recovery program) — total POS recovery ~80%.
🏢
Robust In-house Collections
FTE teams across 50+ locations. Overall collection ops in 200+ locations through agency network. Strong customer engagement model — not dependent on third-party recovery alone.

What's Driving Portfolio Improvement

Better Portfolio Mix
Higher AB share in the portfolio mix driving overall quality improvement. Cohort selection getting sharper.
Vintage Performance Improving
Early and late vintage metrics improving significantly — showing strong underwriting improvement and better collections performance.
Better Sourcing Quality
Flowing from the third moat — two-tiered allocation means cleaner cohorts on own book, better risk-adjusted returns at portfolio level.

Liquidity Management & Co-Lending Model

Transition to co-lending for capital efficiency and scale
Strategic Direction
Over the last year, we have transitioned to a co-lending model to optimize capital efficiency and scale disbursals without proportional balance sheet growth.
The co-lending structure allows us to leverage our distribution and underwriting advantage while sharing credit risk with institutional partners — improving ROE and AUM growth simultaneously.

What-If Scenarios — Managing Macro Cycles

How the ecosystem moats protect us across credit and macro cycles
Scenario: Credit Cycle Tightening
✅ Asset intelligence moat — faster repricing of risk via instant asset valuation (vs 1-day lag for others)
✅ Liquidation moat — 75–78% recovery vs 60% peers buffers NPA impact
✅ GPS on all assets — repossession speed and skip tracing advantage
✅ Two-tier model — shift more originations to T2 / partner books, preserve own-book quality
Scenario: Volume / Macro Slowdown
✅ Distribution moat — captive origination means volumes track our marketplace, not market-wide credit demand
✅ Pre-approval pipeline — 60% PA penetration pre-test-drive gives demand visibility and reduces conversion cost
✅ Buyback integration — ecosystem generates its own supply, insulating from macro inventory shocks
✅ Co-lending flexibility — rapidly adjust own-book exposure based on capital cost and risk appetite
The Core Thesis — Restated
Our structural moats — asset intelligence, distribution control, and liquidation advantage — are not cyclical. They compound over time and protect us in downturns while accelerating returns in upturns. Ecosystem lending is not a bet on credit markets. It's a bet on CARS24's marketplace dominance.
📌 FY26 Recap
🧭 FY27 Theme
🏗️ P1 · Distribution
🔁 P2 · Repeat
⚖️ P3 · Risk

🤖 P5 · AI

FY26 Recap

From FY25 Reset → FY26 Scale
The Headline
FY26 is the year LOANS24 converted the heavy capability investments of FY25 into real, profitable, and controlled growth.
FY25 — Deliberate Reset
Growth moderated to fix fragility from partner-heavy models. Invested in core tech, risk & DS, in-house collections, and treasury independence.
FY26 — Execution
Growth returned after control, not before. Profitability expanded via operating leverage. Risk quality improved while scaling — underwriting maturity confirmed.
FY27 — Compound
End-to-end lending system now operational. Scale is repeatable and predictable. FY27 compounds growth rather than fixing fundamentals.
AUM
₹3,200 Cr
vs ₹2,333 Cr FY25
↑ ~37% YoY
Disbursals
₹6,389 Cr
vs ₹4,389 Cr FY25
↑ ~45% YoY
PBT
~₹35 Cr
vs ~₹9 Cr FY25
↑ ~4× YoY
Opex / Disbursal
~3.5%
vs ~5.0% FY25
↓ Structural
Loss on Fresh Disbursals
~3.7%
vs ~4.7% FY25
↓ 100 bps
Partner Penetration
~40%
Down from ~70%
Volumes held ✓
Growth Quality
Superior
Scale + Control + Profit together
Platform Proven
The Story
Having validated scale, profitability, and control in FY26, LOANS24 enters FY27 positioned to compound growth through distribution, repeat, and capital leverage.
Cost Curve
Bending ↓
Opex declining structurally
Risk Curve
Bending ↓
Losses improving at scale
Capital
Restored
Independence from partners

FY27 Theme — Compounding Growth

Central Strategy
The Headline Message
FY27 is about building a compounding lending platform where growth accelerates through repeat, risk intelligence, and capital leverage — not linear effort. Design systems so growth becomes easier, cheaper, and safer as we scale.
People → Systems
Tech, data, and decision engines scale faster than headcount. Manual execution is the FY26 model — not FY27.
Acquisition → Lifetime Value
Shift from acquisition-led to lifetime-value-led growth. Repeat usage and deeper relationships drive scale.
Balance Sheet → Leverage
Scaling shifts from balance-sheet-led to capital-efficient leverage — predictability unlocks partner capital.

Why Compounding — And Why Now

01
FY26 answered the PMF question
Scale returned, profitability expanded, risk and cost curves improved simultaneously. Partner dependence reduced without volume loss — platform strength confirmed.
Resolved ✓
02
New FY27 constraint: Fragility
Linear acquisition becomes expensive. Marginal risk mistakes amplify. Manual decisions fail to scale. Partner dependency becomes a bottleneck again.
FY27 Challenge
03
Fibe — Risk-First Compounding
~90% lifetime repeat, ~50% disbursals from repeat customers. Underwriting improved every loan cycle. 9 co-lending partnerships created near-marketplace coverage.
BenchmarkConsumer
04
Oxyzo — Distribution-First Compounding
Made credit invisible inside distribution flows. Trade advances → higher WC → longer tenures → adjacent products. Scaled significantly with limited equity.
BenchmarkB2B

Pillar 1 — Distribution-Led Product Platform

Pull-Driven Growth
Headline
FY27 shifts LOANS24 from building distribution for products to building products for the distribution we already have — turning growth from push-driven to pull-driven.
Distribution Is Broader Than Lending
Built through lending and non-lending transactions. Any customer trusting the platform enters the distribution pool.
Products Designed for Distribution
Distribution decides what gets built. Products appear contextually. Solving for "cash need" not selling LAC as-is.
Products Deepen Distribution
Each product must increase frequency, depth, or stickiness. Products are distribution amplifiers, not just revenue events.
FY26 — Products first, distribution second
LAC launched as open-market productForce-fit ✗
DCF failed until inspection was removedWrong fit ✗
Channels treated as distribution to usePush-driven ✗
FY27 — Distribution decides what gets built
Cash financing / PL / pre-approved flowsContextual ✓
TW-based financing for intent-rich baseEmbedded ✓
DIY flows across 24/VI/CI platformsPull-driven ✓

What Success Looks Like

P1 Metrics
📍
Ecosystem Entry Disbursals ↑
% of disbursals from embedded entry points increases materially across platforms
% disbursals via ecosystem entry
🔄
Non-Lending → Lending Conversion
Conversion from non-lending to lending flows improves (e.g. LAC on cash purchases)
Non-lending → lending CVR %
📉
CAC Declines Structurally
CAC for lending products declines without slowing growth. Lower push-spend each quarter.
CAC per disbursal ₹
📦
Products per Active User
More than one lending product per active ecosystem user. Cross-sell depth increases.
Lending products per active user
🏪
Dealer Lead Flow
Lead flow from C2B-onboarded dealers into consumer finance. Dealer trust → lending volume.
% disbursals via dealer channel
🚀
Organic Disbursal Share
Organic share of disbursals (non-paid) increases as distribution flywheel activates.
Organic disbursal share %

Pillar 2 — Repeat-First Lending Engine

Depth Over Width
Headline
In FY27, growth needs to start coming from deepening relationships, not repeatedly reacquiring fresh customers. Repeat is not a metric — it is the business model.
Default Design Goal
Repeat engagement becomes the default across all interactions. One-time borrowing is an entry point, not the end goal.
Known User Experience
Known users get faster decisions, better pricing, higher limits, and lower-friction journeys. Identity and history compound value.
Structured Credit Journey
TW user → small-ticket cash → repayment history → higher limits → used-car financing. Relationship compounds by design.
Fibe Lifetime Repeat Rate
~90%
Across ~9Mn loan accounts
Benchmark
Fibe Repeat Disbursal Share
~50%
Of disbursal volumes from repeat
Benchmark
LOANS24 Early Signal
~₹30 Cr
Own-book via PFL top-ups, minimal friction
Early Evidence

What Success Looks Like

P2 Metrics
🔁
Repeat Disbursal Share ↑
% of disbursals from repeat customers becomes a new primary growth driver across all BUs.
% repeat disbursal share
⚡
PA Disbursal Share ↑
Pre-approved disbursal share as % of total. Known users converting faster at lower friction.
PA disbursal share %
⏱️
Repeat TAT vs New User
Time-to-disbursal improves sharply for known users vs new customers.
Repeat TAT / New user TAT ratio
🏪
Dealer Repeat → Consumer Finance
Dealer repeat drives consumer financing. DCF coverage of repeat dealers increases.
DCF coverage of repeat dealers %

Pillar 3 — Risk-Adjusted Compounding

Predictability Over Profitability
Headline
FY27 growth needs to be governed by predictability, not just profitability. Shift from absolute ROA to risk-adjusted ROA at cohort level.
Cohort-Level Thinking
Decision-making moves from portfolio averages to sub-portfolio and cohort-level risk assessment. Volatile cohorts are priced higher or explicitly capped.
Explicit Risk Caps
Focus on loss variability, tail risk, and vintage stability. Higher-risk cohorts are capped — not scaled indiscriminately.
Optionality, Not Conservatism
Risk-adjusted thinking enables experimentation with sub-prime and unsecured without destabilising core. More options, not fewer.
FY26 Question
Is the overall portfolio profitable?Answered ✓
Can we grow without breaking risk?Answered ✓
FY27 Question
Which cohorts can be scaled safely?FY27 Focus
Which cohorts should be explicitly capped?FY27 Focus

What Success Looks Like

P3 Metrics
📊
Growth in Predictable Cohorts
Growth shifts toward explicitly identified predictable cohorts. Stable cohorts scaled aggressively.
% AUM in rated predictable cohorts
📉
Loss Dispersion Reduces
Volatility reduces even as portfolio expands. Loss dispersion, tail loss, and vintage divergence all narrow.
P90 vs P10 loss rate dispersion
🧪
Controlled Experimentation
Ability to experiment — sub-prime and unsecured — without destabilising core book. Clear stop-losses.
Experiments launched with stop-loss
⚠️
Early Warning Accuracy ↑
EWS detection accuracy improves. Time from signal to policy action decreases materially.
EWS detection accuracy %

Pillar 4 — Capital & Co-Lending Flywheel

Leverage, Not Equity
Headline
FY27 scale will come from capital leverage, not balance-sheet expansion. Synthetic leverage can scale books from ₹1,500 Cr to ₹4,000 Cr at current equity levels.
Own Book → Prove → Leverage
Use own balance sheet to seed and prove performance. Once predictability is established, scale through co-lending not equity.
Co-Lending Is the Reward
Co-lending is not the starting point — it is the reward for predictability. Repeat, risk-adjusted portfolios, and platform maturity unlock it.
LOANS24 Retains Control
Retains control over front-end, data, underwriting logic, and routing. Partners provide capital; LOANS24 owns the relationship.
⚡
Equity Constraints Are Real
Capital allocation across C24 limits NBFC equity. External equity raising is a few quarters out. Synthetic leverage answers the question now.
📈
Scale Potential
Creating synthetic leverage can scale books from ₹1,500 Cr to ₹4,000 Cr+ at current equity levels — without dilution.
🤝
Fibe Playbook
Own book proved performance → co-lending share expanded → equity intensity reduced materially. Predictability unlocked partner capital.

What Success Looks Like

P4 Metrics
📈
AUM Growth Without Proportional Equity
Faster AUM growth while equity deployed as % of AUM declines. Capital efficiency improves materially.
Equity deployed as % of AUM
🏦
Co-Lending Share Increases
Increasing share of stable portfolios funded via co-lending. Co-lending as % of total AUM rises.
Co-lending % of AUM
💰
ROE Improvement
Improved ROE at steady risk levels. Less equity doing more work as leverage kicks in on proven portfolios.
ROE % at stable risk band

Pillar 5 — AI-First Platform

Systems Scale Faster Than Teams
Headline
In FY27, decision systems must scale faster than teams. AI is embedded across underwriting, pricing, routing, and collections. Compounding is structurally impossible without AI-first execution.
Automated Decisioning
Majority of lending decisions through automated systems. Human intervention focused on exceptions and policy design — not execution.
Marginal Cost Declines
As volumes scale, marginal cost per ₹ lent continues to decline. Ops and risk scale without linear headcount growth.
Faster Learning Loops
AI enables faster learning cycles and consistency across products and cohorts. Policy iteration in days, not weeks.
FY26 — Built systems & foundations
Core tech stackBuilt ✓
Data platformsBuilt ✓
Risk & DS capabilityBuilt ✓
FY27 — Let systems actively drive scale
% cases: 1-min approval / disbursal↑ Target
Manual touches per disbursal↓ Target
Policy iteration cycle time↓ Target

What Success Looks Like

P5 Metrics
⚡
Instant Decision Share
% of cases with 1-min approval / disbursal increases materially. Automated decisioning covers majority of volume.
% cases: 1-min approval
👆
Manual Touches Decline
Manual touches per disbursal reduces. Ops headcount grows much slower than disbursal volume.
Manual touches per disbursal
🔬
Faster Policy Iteration
Time to policy change reduces. Early warning detection accuracy improves with better models.
Avg policy iteration cycle time
📊
Ops Efficiency at Scale
Risk and ops scale without proportional headcount. Cost-per-disbursal continues structural decline.
Disbursal volume / FTE

Key Strategic Priorities · FY27

NBFC Level · 8 Priorities
Strategic Intent
Eight cross-cutting priorities governing all BU plans for FY27. Every BU AOP should connect to at least one of these. These are the non-negotiable bets LOANS24 is making this year.
Business Priorities
6
Growth & Revenue
Enabler Priorities
2
Platform & Infrastructure

Business Priorities

Growth
01Growth Priority
Scale Disbursals Profitably Across All Products
Drive disbursement growth across C2C Retail, DCF, Refi, Buyback — with explicit guardrails. Every BU grows, but not at the cost of risk or operating efficiency. Growth quality overrides growth speed.
C2C RetailDCFRefiBuyback
02Repeat Priority
Build Repeat as a Structural Growth Driver
Design every product and journey for repeat. Pre-approved offers, faster repeat TAT, structured credit journeys. Repeat disbursal share becomes a board-level metric in FY27.
All BUsProduct & DS
03Distribution Priority
Deepen Ecosystem Distribution — Embed, Don't Push
Convert the Cars24 ecosystem into a lending distribution machine. DIY flows, contextual PA offers, dealer-led consumer finance, TW financing. CAC must decline structurally this year.
Cars24 EcosystemProductC2C
04Collections Priority
Collections Excellence — From Reactive to Proactive
Build a world-class collections function with early warning systems, bucket-wise strategies, and tech-led follow-up. Collections is a P&L lever, not a back-office function.
CollectionsRiskDS
05Capital Priority
Unlock Co-Lending at Scale — Capital Efficiency This Year
Move from balance-sheet-constrained to capital-efficient. Prove portfolio predictability on own book, then systematically expand co-lending. Equity intensity must reduce in FY27.
TreasuryRiskAll BUs
06Risk Priority
Cohort-Level Risk — Move Beyond Portfolio Averages
Sub-portfolio and cohort-level risk decisioning. Explicit caps on volatile segments. Enable controlled experimentation with sub-prime and unsecured. Predictability governs scale decisions.
RiskDSAll BUs

Platform & Enabler Priorities

Infrastructure
07AI Priority
AI-First Decisioning — Automate the Core Loop
Majority of underwriting, pricing, limits, routing, and collections decisions should be system-driven by end of FY27. Human involvement shifts to exceptions and policy design. Marginal cost per ₹ lent must decline.
ProductDSRisk
08Infra Priority
Regulatory & Compliance Infrastructure — Ready to Scale
Strengthen compliance frameworks, audit readiness, and co-lending norms as the book scales. Proactive regulatory preparedness — not reactive firefighting.
LegalFinanceCompliance

Movement Insights

MoM Signal Analysis
📉
Overall Volume Declining
Peak of ₹10.1 Cr in Nov '25 — fell to ₹7.8 Cr in Feb '26 (▼23%). No channel is compensating for the PhonePe decline. Business is at net contraction despite team activity.
📱
PhonePe — The Core Problem
PhonePe drove 47% of total volume in Nov '25 (₹4.8 Cr). Logins fell from 1,389 to 426 by Feb (▼69%). Volume halved to ₹2.1 Cr. Over-indexing on one channel created systemic fragility.
📈
DIY — Steadiest Growth Trajectory
D2C DIY grew from ₹3.1 Cr (Oct) to ₹4.2 Cr (Jan) — the only channel with consistent MoM growth across 3 consecutive months. Dipped in Feb but remains the most reliable signal.
⚠️
C2B — Volatile, High Drop-offs
Fell sharply in Dec (₹0.73 Cr, 18 disbursals) before recovering in Feb. Approval rate: only 19–23%. Very high logins-to-disbursals ratio confirms CAC problem before the channel was stopped.
🔄
Approval Rates Declining Across Board
Grand total approvals fell from 677 (Oct) to 431 (Feb). Approval rate: 677/2753 = 24.6% → 431/1857 = 23.2%. Even as logins hold, fewer are converting through credit assessment.
🟢
CI/VI — Nascent but Moving
From 3 logins (Oct) to 127 logins (Feb). 5 disbursals, ₹0.18 Cr — tiny in absolute terms but shows intent-filter improvement is generating some signal. Not a volume driver yet.

Experiments & Learnings

FY26 H2 · 14 Experiments across Distribution, PMF, Risk & Partnerships
📡 Distribution
01
Distribution · Channel DIY Superapp — Strategically leverage C24 ecosystem for 8–10 Cr/month LAC 🟡 Partial
Actions / What We Did
Expanded LAC entry points across 5+ surfaces: Homepage, Loans brand page, CX profile page, Bottomsheets, Sell journey. UI & messaging experiments: Cash vs Loan icon, badges (New, Exclusive). Tested placement vs other loan products — removed pill on UCL, exclusivity for LAC.
What Went Well
1.6× uptick in DIY leads from 8.9K → 14.5K/month post go-live. Sustained stable L2D till Feb '26. Grew biz from 1–2 Cr to all-time high of ₹4 Cr/month in Dec '25.
Where We Failed / Gaps
SA traffic declined 8–10% in Jan–Feb driven by reduced perf marketing by CSPL. Funnel breakage in ORBIT: car-fetch failures post UMS integration breaking LAC funnel. Placement optimization still pending within sell journey.
Key Learning
Channel scale is linked to SA traffic & central team dependencies — not fully in our control. Entry points alone (banners/icons) don't drive high-intent ToF. LAC needs clearer category articulation × user need intersection.
02
Distribution · Channel C2B Seller Base — Call Center Approach (Monetize cash-seeking sellers) 🔴 Stopped
Actions / What We Did
Call center-based interest capture initiated Jan '25. Human agent to AI-based intent capture started Dec '25. Target: 5 Cr/month biz from this channel. All-time high of ₹2.5 Cr achieved in Jul–Aug '25 — however CAC was not considered in that milestone.
What Went Well
AI calling learnings: feedback on correct interest capture and right pitch — useful for scaling AI calling to other use cases (drop-off calling, full AI sourcing).
Where We Failed / Gaps
High CAC / cross-charge — needed ₹70L disbursal to break-even vs ₹40L in Jan, ₹30L in Feb. Unit economics never worked. Accountability issues in central team.
Key Learning
C2B cold calling model is structurally unviable at this CAC. Broader auto-IQ base in PA frame is the right continuation — better CX quality, better intent, better OB share.
03
Distribution · CRM C2B Seller Base — CRM Approach (Structured CRM strategy on the same base) 🟢 Active
Actions / What We Did
Structured CRM strategy executed and refined continuously: (1) Initiated with C2B base with data-driven L2A, A2I, I2P insights (I2P showing best L2D of 1.1%); (2) Continuous template iteration — trending at 3.5–4% avg CTR; (3) Transitioned from marketing to utility-based CRM; (4) Deployed learnings to auto-IQ base and PQ base in Feb; (5) Retargeting strategy — good CTR holds even with re-targeting same user.
What Went Well
CAC reduced ~2× while sustaining ~4% CTR across overall base. ~1.6× better L2D for AutoIQ base vs C2B base (0.3% vs 0.5% L2D). CRM outreach capacity scaled from ~5K/day to ~300K/day. Mar '26 CRM strategy at sufficient maturity — cohorts / propensity / templates identified.
Where We Failed / Gaps
OB share from C2B base at 60% but for auto-IQ base only 16% — contributing to top-line but limited bottom-line (4.9% GM for AutoIQ base disbursals).
Key Learning
Continue CRM strategy targeting broader auto-IQ base in PA frame for better CX quality, higher intent, and better OB share. Similar learnings now being leveraged for PA targeting strategy.
04
Distribution · Platform Platform VI / CI — Monetize VI/CI transaction-based traffic; initial target 1 Cr/month 🟠 Learning
Actions / What We Did
Launched unified entry point for cash loan across both VI and CI platforms — routing car owners to LAC, non-car owners to PL via bridge page at Loans24. Further routing of relevant car-owning traffic on RC search page, bottomsheets with eligible car filtering (by year, ownership count, non-EV). OTP bypass for logged-in users. Reg# bypass for registered car owners. Detailed roadmap created analyzing existing apps with high-frequency entry points.
What Went Well
Strong ToF: CI had ~100K page loads on landing page in first month — similar to DIY. Created detailed roadmap for VI/CI strategy. First experiment with bridge page routing — validated concept for product suite routing basis eligibility and need. Two iterations on right product articulation for LAC and PL.
Where We Failed / Gaps
L2D structurally weaker: DIY 0.4% vs CI 0.1% vs VI 0%. Lead to login also same trend: DIY 4.9%, CI 1.3%, VI 0.6%. 40% drop-off at lead creation vs 13% in DIY. Inherent intent problem — having asset-owning customers is not enough.
Key Learning
PA approach is the way forward for VI/CI monetization. High-engagement UPI platforms (PhonePe) are better LAC surfaces than auto-ownership apps. Should have first solved PL as a category in VI (which was already ongoing in Nov) before attempting LAC/PL bridge.
🎯 PMF & Funnel
05
PMF · Customer Research PMF Understanding — Customer calling + bureau scrub on LAC lead base (on-us and off-us) ✅ Done
Actions / What We Did
Evaluated LAC funnel customer behavior through: Bureau scrub conducted on LAC/PL leads (on-us + off-us) for ~1.96L user base. Customer calling done on 100+ customers with results overlaid on their bureau performance. Objective: understand current PMF to make improvements to the offering.
What Went Well
Detailed PMF findings document generated. Key insight: LAC as a category is not intuitive for customers compared to PL/Cash. Speed & ROI matters most to Salaried; credit access & loan amount has highest priority for self-employed.
Where We Failed / Gaps
Full findings in PMF tab. Salaried segment is fundamentally mis-served by current product positioning — we offer worse ROI than PL to our best-credit customers.
Key Learning
LAC as category name is confusing. Speed matters for salaried, amount matters for SE. Separate product articulation and offer design needed per segment.
06
PMF · Pre-Approved Pre-Approved (PA) Program — Target highest quality (profile + asset + intent) customers 🟢 Active
Actions / What We Did
First pilot on PA base of ~50K implemented and underway. Separate PA program construct built with walkthrough. Initial deployment targeted highest quality asset and profile combination. DRE completed with PhonePe on 15L car-owner base for inorganic PA targeting.
What Went Well
Realized CTR of 5%+ including 2% not-interested cohort — strong quality signal. DRE with PhonePe completed — aligned for inorganic targeting further.
Where We Failed / Gaps
Teething issues in product: lead loss in click to page load. Majority of eligible customers found not interested — propensity model not in place. Eligible ≠ interested.
Key Learning
Propensity (willingness for loan) is a critical distinct element from eligibility — needs to be a separate model. This is the primary unlock for FY27 PA program.
07
PMF · Funnel Funnel Efficiency — Experian filter removal at ToF on selected base 🟠 Tested
Actions / What We Did
Removed Experian filter at top of funnel on selected base. Evaluated incremental lead generation without mandatory Experian check at sourcing stage. Additional base of ~3.5K customers tested.
What Went Well
Additional ~500 workable leads generated from the 3.5K base (~14% incremental).
Where We Failed / Gaps
500 additional workable leads led to only 72 logins and just 2 approvals — steep drop in funnel post login. The removal didn't unlock quality leads, just added quantity.
Key Learning
Capturing credit features at ToF for filtering along with risk bucket model features is necessary. Removing filters without a replacement quality signal just adds noise to the funnel.
08
PMF · Conversion Funnel Efficiency — PLL team for no-regret conversion on ABND and dropped cases 🟢 Active
Actions / What We Did
PLL team setup for no-regret conversion offer design on ABND (Approved But Not Disbursed) and potential dropped cases. Initiated analysis of individual customer's CIBIL report using GPT to identify competitive ROI and loan amount. Goal: personalized pitch to each customer based on propensity & existing tradeline analysis.
What Went Well
Additional 3 cases per month disbursed in January from PLL team intervention.
Where We Failed / Gaps
GPT-based CIBIL analysis not yet fully internalized by conversion agents. Individualized pitch basis bureau scrub not yet systematically scaled.
Key Learning
Need individualized pitch to each customer based on propensity and existing tradeline analysis from bureau scrub. This is yet to be fully internalized and automated for conversion agents at scale.
⚖️ Risk & Profitability
09
Risk / Profitability Policy Refinement — Own-book approval rate vs Pmaxx (LAC 12–13% vs C2C/DCF 40–50%) 🟡 Ongoing
Actions / What We Did
Structured daily review of OB rejected cases with credit team. Daily alignment on Pmaxx disbursed cases that were rejected on OB. Objective: bring parity in policy while managing risk. Amber approval rate for LAC at 12–13% vs C2C/DCF at 40–50% — massive gap that needs explaining.
What Went Well
~2 additional OB disbursals per week via daily case discussions on mitigants. Detailed policy benchmarking & recommendations for LAC produced — 3 key priority areas identified.
Where We Failed / Gaps
Daily reviews are not sustainable: increases TAT for CX and absorbs significant operational bandwidth. High subjectivity applied by credit team in individual case decisions. Overall policy relook on most frequent rejection reasons is the right path — not daily manual reviews.
Key Learning
3 key policy areas requiring relook: (1) Allowance for high enquiry customers; (2) Allowance for high unsecured exposure in L3M/L6M; (3) Allowance for bounce in L12M. Structured benchmarking already done — decision is ready.
10
Risk / Profitability Cohort-wise ROI Strategy — Different pricing for Salaried vs Self-Employed 🟡 Ongoing
Actions / What We Did
Higher ROI pitch to self-employed customers compared to salaried during offering design — reflecting the insight that salaried are more ROI-sensitive while self-employed maximize loan amount.
What Went Well
Self-employed customers demonstrated higher ROI tolerance than salaried. SE customers showing higher conversion across risk buckets confirming the insight.
Where We Failed / Gaps
Salaried customers are APR-aware — excessive pricing increase can lead to VAS loss and case drop. The inverse A2D pattern (Bucket A salaried converting worst at 14%) is directly linked to ROI uncompetitiveness.
Key Learning
Self-employed customer needs to be relooked from a product construct/need lens — regular fund requirement, higher loan amount, line-of-credit model. Salaried needs ROI parity with PLs to convert the best-credit customers.
11
Risk / Product Internal Top-up Program — C2C / DCF loan customers offered LAC top-up 🟢 Mar '26 Go-live
Actions / What We Did
Tech-product being groomed for top-up journey — target Mar '26 go-live. Policy draft created and finalization underway. These are existing customers with strong repayment data available for underwriting — high upside relative to effort. Model shows 560 logins, 130 disbursals at ₹8 Cr with 23% L2D.
What Went Well
Highest L2D of any channel at 23% based on model. Zero CAC — customers already in the ecosystem with repayment history. Strong own-book share expected (0% partner share).
Where We Failed / Gaps
Not live yet — go-live is Mar '26. No actual results to validate the model.
Key Learning
This is the single best risk-adjusted incremental bet. Known customer, known car, known repayment history. Low risk, high L2D, zero CAC. Activate aggressively on go-live.
12
Product / Revenue VAS for Refinance Customers — Identify PMF for VAS attach and derive propensity 🟢 Active
Actions / What We Did
Aggressively pushed all VAS products to identify PMF items and derive overall propensity/potential attach. Target: identify which VAS items have right-to-win with refinance customers vs sale-purchase customers (where there's a change in car ownership).
What Went Well
Realized PMF for Refinance VAS items: Pocket Buyback attach rate grew 2× (36% in Dec → 74% in Feb). RSA penetration scaled ~20% (63% → 76% in Feb). Increased VAS prices by ₹500 once attach targets reached.
Where We Failed / Gaps
Overindexing on VAS often leads to case loss — customer's basic requirement is cash, and no 'hook' exists in refinance unlike C2C/DCF transactions. Lower-ticket VAS (₹2,500) with direct multi-fold customer benefits show higher attach rates.
Key Learning
Continue to innovate on pitch. Identify VAS items with lower ticket size (up to ₹2,500) and direct customer benefit — Pocket Insurance, RSA, etc. Don't hard-sell VAS at the cost of case conversion.
🤝 External Partnerships
13
External Partnership PhonePe — Leverage massive payments MAU to build LAC biz of 10 Cr+/month 🟢 Scaling
Actions / What We Did
Journey: Redirection live (Apr '25) → Semi-integration (Oct '25): 1.5× uplift in L2D (0.33% → 0.5%) → Funnel optimization (OGL filtering, loader time P95 7s → <3s, wrong login filter, banking flow fix, asset attach fix): 1.4× further uplift (0.5% → 0.7% L2D) → PQ program on 32L pre-qualified base (L24 as single partner eliminating lender selection page) → DRE completed on 15L car-owner base for PA targeting.
What Went Well
4 Cr+ disbursal from single partner — all-time high. LOANS24 emerged as single LAC redirection partner due to best funnel performance. 14K+ leads/month. OB:Pmaxx ratio of 60:40 — strong own-book quality.
Where We Failed / Gaps
Homepage LAC icon failed entirely at PhonePe product end (Experiment 1 of PQ). Sub-optimal placement (offer strip vs icon). LAC deprioritized vs PL internally due to stronger perceived PL opportunity at PhonePe. Cannot control platform priorities.
Key Learning
LAC should be positioned as one in a suite of 'Cash products' for car-owning segment — not a complete replacement for GL/PL. Building Cash as a unified product safeguards position with partners ensuring no revenue loss.
14
External Partnership CRED — Leverage CRED's strong traffic quality to build LAC biz of 50 Cr+/month ⏸ Blocked
Actions / What We Did
Both biz teams aligned on monthly biz value of 40–50 Cr/month. Both risk teams aligned on key risk metrics and ECL to be factored into pricing. Product workflow discussions initiated. UI/UX learnings used in 1-min PA journey. Learnings on CEX (increasing disbursal % upfront before RC collection, showing real-time dashboard for RC collection process) to be incorporated in BoF charter.
What Went Well
Alignment at business and risk level achieved. UI/UX learnings from CRED discussions incorporated into PA journey design. BoF charter for CEX improvements defined.
Where We Failed / Gaps
Commercial discussion closure pending — CRED not willing to give DLG. Subsequently deprioritized by both teams. 50 Cr/month potential sitting idle on a commercial impasse.
Key Learning
Whitelisting between PL and LAC based on offer strength and customer propensity (tradeline analysis for ROI sensitivity vs higher ticket size requirement) can improve PMF and conversion. Leveraging garage for upfront eligibility checks can make LAC journey frictionless.

🎯 PMF
Policy
13
Increase OB approval share through analysis of rejection reasons and daily reviews with credit team

14

15

Approval rate amber OB%
14% to 20%
₹50–60L / month

₹4 Cr / month

PMF Discovery — Refinance (LAC)

Structured using 4 Fits · Market → Product → Channel → Model
The Question We Started With
Instead of forcing "product–market fit," we dissected market–product fit first.
"Does the product meaningfully solve a recurring problem for a well-defined market segment?"
🏪
Market ↔ Product
Does LAC solve a real, recurring pain for a specific segment?
→
📱
Product ↔ Channel
Does the channel experience match what the segment expects?
→
⚙️
Channel ↔ Model
Does the operating model actually enable the channel promise?
→
💰
Model ↔ Economics
Do unit economics compound with scale?

Fit 1 — Market ↔ Product

The Market is Bifurcated. The Product is Not.
💼
Salaried · Urgent Need Prominence
"Emergency liquidity fallback — not a first-choice product"
Core need: Episodic, reactive — medical bills, family emergencies, lump-sum events
Behavioural anchor: Need disappears once the emergency resolves or any source comes through
Decision variable: ROI (cheapest possible) + Speed (ASAP)
Credit alternatives: Banks underwrite easily — PLs, cards, family first
Hierarchy: Family → Friends → Liquidate assets → PL/cards → Secured loan (last)
The fatal dynamic
Price-sensitive + time-sensitive + intent evaporates. If need vanishes, they vanish. If another source offers 50bps cheaper, they pivot. They're quoting us against a PL they already have at 11–13%. We're quoting 14–16% + hypothecation.
🏪
Self-Employed · Business Need Prominence
"Perennial search for liquidity — this product is the answer, not the fallback"
Core need: Continuous, recurring working capital — not episodic
Behavioural anchor: Need never fully resolves — liquidity search is structural
Decision variable: Loan amount / ticket size — maximize access
Credit alternatives: Harder to underwrite — informal credit exists but costly
Riskier cashflows: Informal income, documentation gaps — banks reject them
Our right-to-win
SE + C/D bucket customers get both higher amounts AND better ROI from us vs their PL history — because they have no PL alternatives to compare us to. We win by access, not by price competition.

Porter's 5 Forces — Why This Matters Strategically

The structural difference between the two markets
ForceSalariedSelf-Employed
Bargaining Power of CustomersHigh — many substitutes (banks, PLs, cards)Low — few options for secured credit
Threat of SubstitutesVery high — instant PLs on appsModerate — informal credit exists but costly
Competitive RivalryIntense — NBFCs, Fintechs, BanksFragmented — high barriers to entry
Supplier Power (Data/Collateral)Low — standardized RCs, stable incomeMedium — documentation gaps
Need UrgencyEpisodic and short-livedContinuous and recurring
Strategic read: We are fighting a high-rivalry, high-substitution war for salaried customers — on their terms. We are the only structured lender for SE customers in this asset class — on our terms.

The Problem — A Structurally Inverted Funnel

Our funnel is literally backwards
What a healthy funnel looks like
Bucket A (best credit) → Converts BEST
Bucket B → Converts well
Bucket C → Converts moderately
Bucket D (marginal) → Converts least
What our funnel actually looks like ↓
Bucket A — 18.9% A2D ← lowest
↙
Bucket B — 37.9% A2D
↑
Bucket C — 36.1% A2D
↑
Bucket D — 51.9% A2D ← highest
↗
Why this inversion exists — the pricing trap
Bucket A Salaried — Why they walk
We offer worse ROI than their PL in 2 of 3 cases. For A-bucket, only 32.8% of our offers beat their best PL. They're getting 11–13% PLs elsewhere. We quote 14–16% + hypothecation. Even when approved, the product is psychologically inferior. They rationally walk away.
Bucket D SE — Why they stay
We offer better ROI AND higher amount than their PL history — because they have no PL alternatives. For D-bucket, 64% of our offers beat their best PL on rate, and 72.7% beat on amount. They stay and disburse because we're the only option that gives them real access to credit.
⚡
Translation
We are cheapest where it matters least (C/D customers who have no alternatives) and costliest where it matters most (A customers with many alternatives). We are positioned opposite to how the customer perceives value.

The Loan Amount Sensitivity Insight

Amount drives conversion — but differently for each segment
Salaried — Amount matters, but ROI breaks it
1–5L bucket~23%
5–7.5L bucket ⚠️ anomaly13.4%
7.5–10L bucket37%
10L+ bucket49%
The 5–7.5L anomaly: Customers in this bracket received worse ROI than their best PL offer — causing an abnormal A2D dip to 13.4%. This confirms: for salaried, ROI competitiveness is a conversion gate, not a nice-to-have.
Self-Employed — Amount dominates, consistently
1–5L bucket~35%
5–7.5L bucket42%
7.5–10L bucket43%
10L+ bucket66%
No anomalies, no dips: SE A2D climbs steadily with loan amount. No ROI-driven breaks. The higher the amount we offer, the more they take it — because access is the constraint, not price.

Fit 2 — Product ↔ Channel

We market a digital product. We deliver a manual journey.
Our acquisition funnels are optimized for instant buyers. Our fulfilment engine runs at human speed.
DimensionSalaried (majority of leads)Self-Employed (converts better)
Need TypeUrgent liquidity / event-drivenOngoing working capital
Purchase TriggerInstant ROI comparisonRelationship trust and negotiation
Channel ExpectationPure digital — instant decisionDigital + offline acceptable
What We Actually DeliverSplit digital + manual journey ✗Same split digital + manual ✗
ResultDrop due to friction and delaySteady conversion via trust
The Product-Channel Mismatch
We are marketing a manual product on digital channels and making a digital promise to customers who receive a manual delivery. Until our channels mirror our segment fit, our A2D will stay structurally capped.

Fit 3 — Channel ↔ Model

Designed for Control, Not for Velocity
Our operating model was built for underwriting confidence, not for conversion velocity. Top-of-funnel experience looks digital; back-end machinery behaves legacy. After approval, every salaried user enters a semi-manual fulfilment chain involving RC inspection, valuation, document collection, and HPA filing — each owned by a different micro-team.
Sales team
Measured on: Approvals
Drives: Over-selling to low-fit customers
Outcome: High drop post-approval
Operations
Measured on: Compliance TAT & Risk
Drives: Redundant checks, overloads customer early
Outcome: High friction, delay, rework
Credit
Measured on: NPA rate
Drives: Defensive risk stance across all cohorts
Outcome: Under-approve prime digital customers
Product
Measured on: Feature release velocity
Drives: Short-term optimisations for monthly disbursal boost
Outcome: Short-term wins only, no compounding

PMF Matrix — Where We Win & Where We Don't

Friction vs Credit Scarcity
High Credit Scarcity · Low Friction
🏆 Sweet Spot — SE, C/D buckets
Self-employed customers in B–D buckets with smooth journey. They have no alternatives, maximize on loan amount, and our ROI IS competitive. These customers stay, convert, and are our right-to-win segment.
High Credit Scarcity · High Friction
⚠️ Risk — SE customers we lose to ops
Self-employed customers who want to disburse but are lost to documentation complexity, RC issues, or HPA delays. These are salvageable — reducing operational friction directly adds disbursals in this quadrant.
Low Credit Scarcity · High Friction
🚪 Dead Zone — Salaried, A/B bucket
Salaried Bucket A customers who have abundant cheaper PL alternatives AND face our manual, high-friction journey. This is where most of our approvals sit and why most walk. We're competing on our weakest dimensions.
Low Credit Scarcity · Low Friction
🎯 Opportunity — STP for salaried
If we fix the journey to be fully digital and fix ROI for Bucket A — this quadrant opens up. Salaried customers with great credit who get a fast, cheap offer will convert. This is the STP-first investment thesis.

Recommendations

From Diagnosis to Action
🏆
Double Down on Self-Employed
Leverage lower ROI sensitivity via higher ticket-size loan and line-of-credit offerings. SE is our right-to-win segment — they have no alternatives and our access advantage is real.
Target SE A2D: 40%+
💰
Fix ROI Competitiveness for Salaried A
Best-credit customers getting worse ROI than PL in 2 of 3 cases. Beat PL on rate for Bucket A, or they will continue to rationally walk away every single time.
Beat PL ROI for 50%+ of Bucket A
⚡
Build Fully Digital Journey for Salaried
Marketing a digital product but delivering a manual journey. Salaried need speed — every manual step post-approval is a rational exit point. STP must be the default, not the exception.
Salaried STP target: <10 mins
📊
Segment-Specific Offers — Stop One-Size-Fits-All
SE → maximize loan amount pitch. Salaried → competitive ROI + speed pitch. Different decision variables require fundamentally different offer design and communication.
Segment CVR uplift target: +30%
📐
Fix Incentive Misalignment in the Model
Sales measured on approvals → over-sells. Credit measured on NPA → under-approves prime digital. Ops measured on compliance TAT → overloads customer. Realign KPIs around conversion and disbursals.
KPI redesign across Sales, Ops, Credit
🔄
Monthly ROI vs PL Benchmarking
ROI and TAT vs leading PL players must be a monthly competitive scorecard. Differentiation cannot be assumed — it must be continuously validated and actively maintained.
Monthly parity tracking cadence

Strategic Reset — QOP FY27

3-Month Retrospective · Structural Diagnosis · Focus Pivot
Executive Summary
The business did not stall because of lack of effort.
It stalled because:
→ We behaved like a scaled multi-engine platform before validating a primary engine.
→ We ran parallel experiments across too many dimensions.
→ We optimized weekly funnel breaks instead of declaring a dominant constraint.
→ We did not explicitly choose what business we are building.
🔥
High Activity
14 experiments in 5 months
📡
Fragmented Signal
No dominant constraint declared
🔴
₹10 Cr Plateau
No MoM growth for 3 months
⚙️
No Compounding Engine
Many loops, none compounding
This QOP is not about improving execution across all fronts.
It is about correcting focus, sequencing, and strategic identity.

What Went Wrong — Structural Diagnosis

4 Root Causes
Root Cause 1 — Too Many Battles Simultaneously
In one quarter, we worked on all of the following simultaneously:
DIY funnel optimization
Assisted conversion scale
CRM channel scaling
PA experiments
PhonePe / CRED integrations
Credit policy relaxations
Own-book push
VAS experiments
C2B cold calling
Unified cash thinking
Each initiative
Made sense in isolation. Had a valid hypothesis. Had bandwidth allocated to it.
Collectively
Fragmented attention. At ~10 Cr/month, we are not a diversified lending portfolio — we are still in primary engine validation stage. We operated like a multi-engine platform before stabilizing one engine.
Root Cause 2 — Optimized Local Bottlenecks Instead of Declaring the Core Constraint
Every time something dipped, we reacted:
Login dropped→ fix tech
Approval dropped→ tweak policy
Disbursal lag→ tweak ops
Volume low→ open partner
CAC high→ try CRM
OB share low→ tweak risk
What we never asked
"What is the single dominant constraint of this business right now?"
Without that clarity, solving one bottleneck created another. This created the recurring feeling that "Something always keeps breaking."
In reality: The system has too many moving parts for its maturity stage.
Root Cause 3 — Strategic Ambiguity: We Didn't Decide What Business We Are
Over the quarter, we oscillated between four identities. Each requires a fundamentally different operating model.
🏦
LAC Originator
Asset-backed, own-book heavy
🔗
PL Marketplace
Distribution-led, asset-light
💰
Unified Cash Platform
Suite of products, bundled journey
👥
Assisted Conversion Biz
Human-led, ops-intensive
The conflicts this created
→ Credit priorities conflicted with distribution goals
→ Own-book goals conflicted with partner flexibility
→ Assisted ops conflicted with STP ambition
→ LAC and PL competed for the same team's attention
The fix
The problem wasn't effort. It was lack of declared focus. This QOP declares one identity: PA-led, STP-first Cash engine.
Root Cause 4 — We Confused Experimentation with Focus
We ran many pilots. We experimented horizontally across channels, products, ops, credit, and partners.
✓
We learned many things — valid signal across experiments
✗
We didn't compound one thing — no primary engine strengthened
Pilots compound only when:
1
There is a declared primary engine
2
Everything feeds into strengthening that engine

How We Should Think About the Last 3 Months

3 Truths Revealed
The last 3 months were not wasted. They revealed three important truths:
🎯
Truth 1 — Intent quality > volume
Broad funnel traffic is not enough. 100K CI page loads → near-zero L2D. Intent quality matters more than quantity. We cannot buy our way to conversion with volume alone.
👥
Truth 2 — Assisted scale without inbound quality is expensive
Human-led conversion cannot compensate for weak cohorts. Assisted ops becomes a cost center, not a value center, when the top-of-funnel intent is low.
✅
Truth 3 — PA cohorts show the cleanest path
Pre-approved cohorts show higher intent, lower ops load, and cleaner funnel metrics. The issue was not wrong direction — it was wrong sequencing and excessive surface area.

The Real Diagnosis

It's not a scale problem
At 10 Cr/month, we are not facing a scale problem.
We are facing a primary engine validation problem.
Brings
High-intent customers only
Converts
Predictably with stable L2D
Produces
Clean unit economics
Improves
With scale — not degrades
Until that loop exists, diversification creates noise — not growth.

The Strategic Pivot — Next 90 Days

We need a focus pivot, not a product pivot
From — Multi-front Experimentation
ApproachParallel experiments across all dimensions
Volume mindsetVolume hunting — more leads = more disbursals
Product strategyLAC and PL as separate parallel growth tracks
Ops modelAssisted-heavy as core growth driver
Channel strategyMulti-channel expansion simultaneously
To — Single Dominant Growth Thesis
ApproachOne primary engine: PA-led, STP-first Cash
Volume mindsetIntent curation — filter before any spend
Product strategy"Cash Router" — one journey, internal routing
Ops modelSTP-first; assisted = salvage only
Channel strategyPA + propensity filter first; expand after validation

3 Operational Pivots

What changes in practice
🎯
Pivot 1 — Volume Hunting → Intent Curation
Stop asking: "How do we increase leads?"
Start asking: "How do we only allow high-probability cohorts into the engine?"
PA and propensity filtering become central to every single channel decision.
Practical change
No channel investment without a propensity filter in place. Every ToF decision starts with "what is the intent signal of this cohort?"
💳
Pivot 2 — Product Push → Cash Router
Instead of debating LAC vs PL, build a single "Instant Cash" journey. The system decides internally:
→ Asset-backed eligible: LAC fast track
→ Not eligible: PL marketplace
→ Rejected LAC but bureau strong: PL fallback
User sees one product: Cash. We route internally.
Practical change
One product brief. One funnel. One set of metrics. PL is tested inside the PA-led funnel — not as a separate growth thesis or competing priority.
🤖
Pivot 3 — Assisted-heavy → STP-first
STP must become the primary engine — not assisted. Assisted becomes:
→ Salvage layer for dropped STP cases
→ Edge-case handling for complex situations
→ High-value, complex case management
It is not the core growth driver.
Practical change
Ops bandwidth shifts to exception management and STP quality improvement — not volume execution. Sales KPI moves from approvals to disbursals.

What We Explicitly Pause — Next 90 Days

Deliberate choices, not failures
⏸ Explicitly Paused
⏸
Broad PL distribution expansion
⏸
Multiple partner integrations simultaneously
⏸
Aggressive C2B scale
⏸
VAS optimization before core funnel stabilizes
⏸
Simultaneous LAC and PL parallel scaling
✓ Important Clarification
PL should not be ignored.
But it should be tested inside the same PA-led funnel, not as a separate growth thesis.

These are deliberate pauses — not failures or permanent pivots. Each initiative will be revisited once the primary engine is validated and stable.

Prioritization Hierarchy

This sequence must not be inverted
1
System Stability
Fix tech leakage and funnel reliability. Until stable, no investment in ToF growth makes sense — you're pouring into a leaky bucket.
Foundation
2
Intent Quality
Build propensity scoring + expand PA program for the diamond cohort. This is the key unlock for PA, PhonePe DRE, CRED, CRM retargeting — all depend on this single model.
Primary Unlock
3
Conversion Engine
Tightly optimized STP journey for the PA + propensity cohort. Minimize manual touches. Measure every drop post-approval and eliminate systematically.
Scale Engine
4
Unit Economics
Controlled credit A/B experiments. Own-book guardrails. Segment-specific pricing (SE vs salaried). Validate CAC, NIM, and OB loss rate for the primary cohort.
Validate
5
Expansion
PL marketplace scaling + CRED integration + broader partner channels. Only after 1–4 are confirmed. Expansion without validated unit economics only adds noise.
After 1–4

What Success Looks Like After 90 Days

5 questions we must be able to answer
At the end of this QOP cycle, we should be able to say with confidence:
1
We know our highest-converting cohort.
Profile, asset quality, intent signal, propensity score — documented and reproducible.
2
We know stable L2D for that cohort.
Stable lead-to-disbursal rate across at least 2 consecutive months. Not a one-month spike.
3
We know true CAC.
Fully loaded CAC including all channel costs, ops, and partner fees. No hidden costs.
4
We know OB economics viability.
Own-book ROA, NIM, and loss rate for the PA-led STP cohort confirmed. Viability at scale tested.
5
We know if this engine scales.
If we double the PA cohort size, does L2D hold? Does ops handle the load? Does cost scale sub-linearly?
⛔ Gate: If we cannot answer these clearly, expansion will only add noise.
This sequence must not be inverted under pressure to hit volume numbers.

Best Demonstrated Performance + June Target

Channel-wise · Monthly · ₹ Cr
How to read this
BDP (Best Demonstrated Performance) is the highest monthly volume each channel has achieved — not the average. The June target reflects the PA program becoming the primary growth driver. Most other channels continue at BAU trajectory. The PA program + Internal Top-up together are the new incremental bets.
CategoryChannelBDP (₹Cr)BDP MonthLogins (BDP)DisbursalsL2D %June Target
DIY Organic
C24 Superapp LACCars24 organic surfaces4.21Jan '26963737.6%5.0
C24 Superapp PLCars24 PL flow3.0Steady———2.0
CI / VIVahan + Cars Inspekt0.18Feb '2612753.9%1.0
Inorganic / Intent-led
C2B Base (CRM)CRM retargeting on AutoIQ2.58Feb '26575468.0%3.0
PA Program 🆕Pre-approved + Propensity—New———4.0
PQ ProgramPre-qualified program—New———2.0
Internal Top-up 🆕C2C/DCF customers · Mar go-live8.0Model56013023%5.0
External Partnerships
PhonePeSemi-integrated · PQ program live4.80Nov '251,2221078.8%5.0
PL PA w/ PartnersPartner PA program6.5Current———3.0
CREDBlocked — DLG impasse—————TBD
OverallBDP: ₹10.1CrNov '25~30 Cr

The FY27 Incremental Bet

Where the growth comes from
BAU Channels (~₹15–18 Cr)
DIY + PhonePe + C2B CRM + PL PA
These channels continue at current trajectory — optimize but do not over-invest. Focus is on protecting the L2D and fixing funnel leakage rather than scaling ToF.
Primary Bet — PA Program (~₹4–6 Cr new)
PA + Propensity → STP Journey
The entire focus of this QOP. Build propensity scoring, validate diamond cohort L2D, confirm OB economics. If this works, it becomes the primary compounding engine — not a supplementary channel.
Quick Win — Internal Top-up (~₹5–8 Cr new)
Existing C2C/DCF Customers
Highest L2D at 23%. Zero CAC. Already known customers with repayment history. Tech go-live Mar '26. This is the lowest-risk incremental volume available — activate aggressively.
Status: ✓ Have ⟳ In Progress + Need to Build ◈ Need to Define ⊘ Deprioritized
🚗 GTM FRAMEWORK · LAC AS PROXY PL
Loan Against Car — Pre-Approved Distribution Program
End-to-end program blueprint across Cars24, VehicleInfo & CarInfo. Four-stage framework.
₹5,400 Cr+
Total Ecosystem TAM
70%
Users bureau 700+
20–30% PA
Expected PA rate (+30–40% PQ)
80%
PV ↔ customer mapping
Stage 1 · TAM
~70%
TAM sized, bureau mapped
Stage 2 · Offers
~50%
OBV live, CIBIL deal done
Stage 3 · Reach
~30%
PhonePe live, AI calling next
Stage 4 · Conversion
~35%
BRE live, BOF 4–6 weeks
💡 Key Signal
PL dominates credit demand (~8.8% C24, ~27% VI, ~24% CI). LMV owners show meaningful GL appetite (4.1%), validating LAC as a compelling PL substitute. Mid-prime (700–800) drives ~65% of TAM — the primary targeting cohort.
1TAM & Market IntelligenceUnderstand the lending opportunity across C24, VI, CI ecosystems · Readiness ~70%
▶
Demand & Sizing
📊
Ecosystem TAM Validation (C24, VI, CI)
₹2,200 Cr C24 / ₹1,250 Cr CI / ₹1,950 Cr VI. Platform-level sizing complete.
✓ Have
📈
Loan Incidence by Product (PL, STPL, GL, BL)
Product-level demand mapped across all 3 platforms with incidence rates and platform-level variations.
✓ Have
🏦
Bureau Score Distribution (Credit Quality)
~70% users at 700+. Experian (C24) vs CIBIL (CI/VI) segmented. Band-wise TAM contribution mapped.
✓ Have
🚗
Vehicle Coverage & User Mix (Veh360)
~56% users mapped to vehicles. LMV ~30%, 2W ~23%. CI highest at 67% coverage.
✓ Have
🔍
Loan Behaviour by Vehicle Segment (LMV)
PL 4.32%, GL 4.1%, BL 0.47% within LMV owners. STPL under-indexes vs. overall base.
✓ Have
🎯
Offer Penetration Proxy (FIBE / C24)
43% of C24 sample stamped with PA offers. Product-wise incidence delta vs non-stamped base observed.
✓ Have — C24 only
Gaps to Address
🔗
Cross-Platform Overlap & Deduplication
Adjust TAM for user overlap across C24/CI/VI. Scenario-based dedup model for true addressable universe. Dashboard provision exists but not applied.
+ Need to Build
💎
Customer LTV & Monetisation Potential Model
Estimate revenue per customer across repeat lending, insurance, and cross-sell to prioritise acquisition spend by cohort.
+ Need to Build
🏗️ Core Thesis
Car↔customer mapping + bureau + income + vehicle value unblocked = PA offers for 20–30% of mapped universe. Additional 30–40% reachable via PQ with softer bureau thresholds and customer-provided income validation.
Vehicle Universe Funnel — Addressable Base
Filter <120 Days <180 Days <360 Days ★ 360+ Days

2Offer Creation & UnderwritingBuild data, model & policy stack for PA/PQ offer generation · Readiness ~50%
▶
Data Inputs
🔑
Car ↔ Customer Mapping (Veh360)
~80% of PVs in India mapped. RC-level ownership linkage to mobile/profile available.
✓ Have
🚘
Vehicle Online Valuation (OBV / C24 Model)
End-to-end vehicle pricing model in place. Forms basis for LTV calculation on LAC offers.
✓ Have
👤
Customer Profile Data (DOB, Gender, PAN, Address)
KYC-level identifiers needed for underwriting. Currently only mobile number available.
▶ Next: Deal done with 3rd party for 3Cr PAN and profile parameters
+ Need to Buy / Enrich
📋
Bureau Scrub — CIBIL Pull
CIBIL integration for pre-offer scoring. Experian already available for C24 base.
▶ Next: Recurring deal done for scrub at 45-day latency
⟳ In Progress
💵
Inhouse Income Estimation Model
Surrogate income model using car value, profile signals, and behavioural proxies. Core to EMI sizing and credit decisioning.
▶ Next: 55% accuracy achieved. To be improved with further vehicle proxy data
⟳ In Progress
🔒
Vehicle Encumbrance & Eligibility Check
Active loan check on car (Parivahan), vehicle age, RC validity, and ownership continuity for LAC eligibility.
▶ Next: Pending from CI/VI data — closure by next week
+ Need to Build
Risk & Pricing Policy
📐
Risk Policy & Credit Scorecard (UCL Proxy → PL Scorecard)
Using UCL underwriting as bridge. Custom PL-LAC scorecard to be developed on bureau + income + vehicle signals.
▶ Next: Scorecard to be built post DS platformization (4 weeks). Leverage UCL scorecard till then
⟳ In Progress
📏
LTV Policy & Loan Sizing Framework
LTV bands, max loan amount caps by segment, and minimum vehicle value thresholds defined.
✓ Have
💲
Pricing Engine — Interest Rate Bands
Risk-based pricing model mapping score bands to IRR ranges. Competitive vs PL for target segments.
✓ Have
🛡️
Fraud Detection & Negative List Policy
Flag synthetic identities, RC mismatch, known defaulters, and suspicious mobile-car pairing.
▶ Next: Part of 5-min disbursal frame
+ Need to Build
Offer Generation
✅
Pre-Approved (PA) Offer Engine — Target 20–30%
Full PA where bureau is strong + income validated + car mapped + no encumbrance. No-doc approval path.
⟳ In Progress
🔄
Pre-Qualified (PQ) Offer Engine — Target 30–40%
Softer offers where bureau or income has gaps. Customer provides income proof or self-declaration to unlock full approval.
+ Need to Build
3Customer Reach & DistributionRight offer to right customer — existing ecosystem and new-to-platform · Readiness ~30%
▶
📡 Key Frame
Existing-to-ecosystem requires compliance-first reach (clean for C24, needs work for CI/VI) + propensity model to avoid spray. New-to-ecosystem is partnership-led — PhonePe live, CRED PA redirect planned. CAC must be tracked against LTV per cohort.
Existing Ecosystem — Reachability
📜
Consent & Compliance Framework — C24
Clean consent stack available for Cars24 customers. CRM reachability confirmed for lending outreach.
✓ Have
📜
Consent & Compliance Framework — CI & VI
Consent for lending outreach not yet established. Requires legal review and DPA-compliant data sharing agreements.
+ Need to Build
Existing Ecosystem — Targeting
🧠
Loan Propensity Model (High-Intent Scoring)
ML model to score likelihood of loan uptake using behavioural, search, and transactional signals. Prevents wasteful broad outreach.
▶ Next: AI-based first cut model ready, to be tested
+ Need to Build
🎛️
Segmentation & Cohort Targeting Framework
Define priority cohorts: high-value LMV owners, recent buyers, users with demonstrated credit intent.
▶ Next: AI-based first cut model ready — being tested in internal top-up
+ Need to Build
Existing Ecosystem — Channel Strategy
📧
CRM Outreach (Email, SMS, WhatsApp)
Off-platform CRM channels active for C24. Need to templatise LAC-specific offer communication and test open/conversion rates.
⟳ In Progress — C24
🤖
AI Calling / Voicebot Outreach
Automated voice-first channel for offer nudging and intent capture. High ROI for lending.
▶ Next: Going live next week
⟳ In Progress
📱
On-Platform Embedded Journeys
In-app offer surfacing during high-intent moments (post-listing, post-valuation, post-service booking).
⊘ Deprioritized
🔔
Push Notifications
App-level push for offer alerts active on C24. Needs personalisation with loan amount and rate to improve CTR.
⟳ In Progress
New to Ecosystem — Distribution
🤝
Platform Partnership — PhonePe
Overlap-based distribution with PhonePe ETB base live. Offers surfaced to matched car-owning users.
✓ Have — Live
🤝
Platform Partnership — CRED & Others
High-quality credit-aware users — strong match for prime+ LAC offers. Expand to similar fintech partners.
▶ Next: PA-based redirection planned
⟳ In Planning
🌐
Platform Entry — Offer Landing for Organic Users
Pre-built offer page for users who discover LAC organically (SEO, referral, partner redirect).
⊘ Deprioritized
📣
Performance Marketing — Paid Acquisition
Targeted digital ads for car owners (Google, Meta custom audiences using RC data).
⊘ Deprioritized
Guardrails & Measurement
💸
CAC Model & Cost Guardrails per Channel
Set max CAC thresholds per cohort (PA vs PQ, prime vs sub-prime) and enforce channel-wise spend limits vs. loan unit economics.
◈ Need to Define
📊
Attribution & Campaign Tracking
Measure channel contribution to application and disbursement. Prevent double-counting across CRM, push, partner, and paid channels.
+ Need to Build
🧪
A/B Testing & Message Optimisation Framework
Test offer framing (amount-led vs rate-led vs tenure-led), channel mix, and timing. Systematise learning loops.
+ Need to Build
🔁
Re-Targeting & Re-Engagement Flows
Automated nurture for users who viewed offer but did not apply. Time-bound nudge sequences across channels.
+ Need to Build
4Conversion & FulfilmentPL-like experience from offer acceptance to disbursement · Readiness ~35%
▶
⚡ Design Principle
Customer sees this as a personal loan — fast, digital, minimal documents. LAC complexity (vehicle hypothecation, RC endorsement) absorbed in the backend. Target offer-to-disbursal under 48 hours. Post-BRE approval must reach 90%+ to avoid funnel leakage.
Decisioning & Offer Finalisation
⚙️
Business Rule Engine (BRE) — Real-Time Decisioning
Automated BRE running lender policy + internal scorecard checks at application time. PA-to-final-approval flow live.
✓ Have
🎯
Final Offer Approval — Target 90%+ Post BRE
Minimise rejection at final stage by tuning PA offer eligibility tightly upfront.
▶ Next: Currently at 50%, to be refined as scorecard and data inputs mature
⟳ In Progress
🏷️
Offer Presentation & Pricing Levers
Personalised offer (amount, rate, tenure, EMI) with customer-driven adjustments (loan amount slider, tenure toggle) live.
✓ Have
➕
VAS & Cross-Sell at Offer Stage
Credit protect insurance, car insurance, top-up eligibility, roadside assistance bundling to improve unit economics.
+ Need to Build
BOF Journey — KYC, Documentation & Disbursement
🚀
5-Minute Disbursal Journey — Full BOF Stack
Single unified flow: digital KYC (Aadhaar eKYC / V-KYC), RC auto-populated from Veh360, income proof via Account Aggregator, e-signed loan agreement with vehicle hypothecation clause, lender API routing, same-day disbursement, NACH/UPI AutoPay mandate. Entire BOF built as one PL-like experience.
▶ Next: BOF journey being built as part of 5-min disbursal program. Full stack target readiness: 4–6 weeks
⟳ In Progress — 4–6 weeks
Post-Disbursement & Servicing
⭐
Post-Disbursement Experience & NPS
Statement access, part-prepayment, repayment reminders, and NPS loop. Critical for repeat borrowing and ecosystem trust.
+ Need to Build
📉
Collections Strategy & Early Delinquency Framework
DPD buckets, collection ownership (lender vs Cars24), digital-first nudges, escalation protocol. Vehicle repossession nuance for LAC.
◈ Need to Define
🎧
Customer Support & Grievance Redressal
Dedicated lending support flow. Nodal officer designation, grievance TAT compliance per RBI LSP norms.
+ Need to Build
Critical Path
Customer profile enrichment (deal done) + CIBIL at scale (deal done, 45-day) → income model → tighten scorecard post DS platformization (4 weeks) → BOF 5-min disbursal live (4–6 weeks) → refine final approval rate 50% → 90%+ → then open reach channels at scale.
⚠️ Opening distribution before underwriting is solid will burn consent and customer trust.
🧭 Philosophy & Themes
🏛️ Projects by Pillar
📐 Pecking Order
⭐ North Stars
🛠️ All Projects

Philosophy & Themes — FY27

Data Science · Annual Operating Plan
Transformation Mission
We are transforming from a traditional DS function into an AI-native, self-learning credit intelligence enterprise.
Every FY27 DS investment is sequenced to deliver this transformation: durable AI infrastructure first, then predictable repeat lending, then capital-efficient scale via co-lending. No optimization before the platform. No expansion before the engine.

Two P0 Priorities That Define FY27

Everything else follows from these two themes
🎯
P0 Theme 1
PA Enablement
Build the intelligence and signals needed to make Pre-Approved programs work at scale. Without a propensity model, a real-time intent signal, and cashflow-backed eligibility — the PA program cannot compound.
🔗
Pre-Approved Ecosystem Layer
Always-on PA surfaces across all touchpoints
💡
Embedded Intent Intelligence
Real-time intent sensing to power PA targeting
🏦
Account Aggregator / Cashflow Ingestion
Real-time income data for SE eligibility unlock
🧠
Behavioral Risk Models / Propensity Scoring
Willingness-to-borrow model — the PA diamond cohort filter
🏗️
P0 Theme 2
Platform for Capability
Build the self-learning model infrastructure that makes all future DS work faster, safer, and compoundable. A driverless ML platform and AI-native workflows remove the manual bottlenecks that slow model deployment and iteration cycles.
⚙️
Driverless ML Platform
Feature store + auto-retrain + deployment — the model factory
🤖
GenAI & Automation
AI-embedded workflows for decisioning, pitch personalization, ops
Why first: Every future DS project (N=1, ARIM, CLTV, capital scoring) runs better and deploys faster when the platform infrastructure is live. Skipping this means rebuilding for every project — compounding tech debt.

The 5-Layer Investment Architecture

Sequence matters — each layer enables the next
🏗️
Layer 1 — Foundation
Driverless ML, AA/cashflow, GenAI, alt-data
Models are reliable, auditable and deployable at scale. Self-learning infrastructure removes manual deployment bottlenecks. The platform factory — invest first.
🧠
Layer 2 — Intelligence
Behavioral risk, N=1 scoring, predictive risk
Convert every product touchpoint into repeat, predictable cohorts. Propensity scoring is the PA diamond cohort filter — this is the primary unlock for FY27 disbursals.
📱
Layer 3 — Product & Distribution
PA ecosystem, embedded intent, front-door APIs
Turn predictability into compounding disbursals. Pre-approval surfaces at every touchpoint. Intent-sensing that routes customers before they even start an application.
⚙️
Layer 4 — Optimization & Ops
CLTV, collections ML, experimentation
Close the loop quickly. Once the engine is validated, optimization compounds returns — higher CLTV, lower loss, faster experiment cycles.
🏦
Layer 5 — Capital Enablement
Co-lender scoring, cohort reporting, ECL
Scale via third-party funding. Predictability unlocks co-lending — cohort packaging and portfolio scoring outputs make AUM bankable to institutional partners.

Pecking Order — Execution Waves

3 Waves · Platform → Engine → Scale
P0 builds the PA engine and the self-learning platform. P1 adds signal depth and capital readiness. P2 scales distribution and optimization once infrastructure and risk controls demonstrate stability. MRM and ARIM move to P2 — monitoring infra follows after the platform itself is live.
P0
Immediate — Start Now
🎯 PA Enablement 🏗️ Platform Capability
🎯 PA Enablement
Pre-Approved Ecosystem Layer
Always-on pre-approvals. ≥35% of disbursals via PA pipeline.
Embedded Intent Intelligence
Real-time intent sensing for PA targeting and funnel routing.
Account Aggregator / Cashflow Ingestion
Real-time income verification. ≥30% of disbursals using AA signals.
Behavioral Risk Models / Propensity Scoring
Willingness-to-borrow model — the PA diamond cohort filter.
🏗️ Platform Capability
Driverless ML Platform
Feature store + automated retrain pipelines. ≥75% of production models on platform. ≥4 auto retrains/month.
GenAI & Automation
AI-embedded workflows for credit decisioning, pitch personalization, and ops automation.
Why Platform at P0
Every P1 and P2 project (N=1, ARIM, CLTV) runs faster and deploys safer when the driverless platform is live. MRM and ARIM monitoring move to P2 — after the platform itself is stable.
P1
Within 3 Months — Signal Depth & Capital Readiness
Intelligence
N=1 / Unified Customer Lifetime Score
Single score per customer across all products. ≥40% of repeat disbursals routed using N=1 signals.
Capital
Portfolio Predictability & Capital Scoring
≥40% of AUM in predictable cohorts for co-lending. Turns data into bankable capital arguments.
Foundation
Alternate Data Curation v2
≥25 validated alternate features. ≥50% of new models leverage alt-data. Expands signal beyond bureau.
Distribution
Fast Fraud & Eligibility Models
Instant eligibility at ToF. Reduces ghost leads and funnel leakage before any credit assessment.
P2
~6 Months — Scale Distribution, Optimization & Risk Monitoring
Includes MRM/Monitoring and ARIM — moved here because monitoring infrastructure should follow the platform being live, not precede it.
MRM / Monitoring Infra ARIM & Early Warning Models Risk-aware acquisition filters CLTV optimization DS Collections (ML-prioritized) ECL & vintage stability Standardized cohort reporting Packaging PA cohorts for partners Capital dashboards & scorecards Co-lender routing engine Reusable front-door APIs Cohort definitions for partners
